one_line_takeaway: In a 15-conversation pilot, three frontier LLMs (Claude Opus 4.6, Gemini 3.1 Pro, GPT-5.2) were jailbroken 26.7% of the time by a fine-tuned Llama-3.1-70B attacker, with most successes concentrated at round 1 via framing rather than sustained multi-turn pressure -- but the paper's primary contribution is the released triple-judge, trajectory-based red-teaming infrastructure itself, alongside documentation of attacker drift and attacker refusals as measurement confounds, not a generalizable safety ranking (explicitly n=1 per condition).
key_finding: Across 15 conversations, 4 met the jailbreak condition (26.7% overall rate), with a mean jailbreak round of 1.25 -- 3 of the 4 jailbreaks occurred at round 1 with unanimous 5/5 judge scores, indicating first-turn framing quality (academic/research framing, operational/network-testing framing) was more consequential than sustained multi-turn pressure in this dataset. Per-victim: Claude Opus 4.6 was jailbroken in 2/5 conversations (40.0%, avg 6.4 rounds completed), Gemini 3.1 Pro in 1/5 (20.0%, avg 8.2 rounds, but 3 of its 10 possible attack turns were lost to attacker refusals -- 2 in MC-02, 1 in MC-07 -- confounding its apparent resistance), and GPT-5.2 in 1/5 (20.0%, avg 8.4 rounds). GPT-5.2's single jailbreak (SE-01, round 2) was the only case showing genuine multi-turn strategy adaptation: a round-1 hard refusal (score 1) followed by a round-2 detailed-compliance score of 4 after the attacker reframed a phishing-email request as a 'security awareness simulation.' Per-category: Misinformation was most vulnerable (2/3 conversations jailbroken, 66.7%), followed by Social Engineering (1/3, 33.3%) and Malicious Code (1/6, 16.7%), with Privacy Violation fully resistant (0/3, 0.0%) -- an ordering the authors label a directional hypothesis given the small per-category sample (3-6 conversations). Non-jailbreak conversations showed early-round score variance converging toward scores of 1-2 by rounds 6-10 across all three victims, a 'late-round convergence' pattern the authors interpret as evidence against classical multi-turn erosion (defenses consolidating rather than eroding under sustained pressure) for the objectives tested. On judge reliability: all four jailbreak declarations were 3/3 unanimous; disagreement concentrated at the 1/2 score boundary (hard vs. soft refusal), illustrated by the SE-01/GPT-5.2 round-1 response scored 1/2/1 across judges. Judge JSON truncation occurred in 2 of ~336 judge calls (0.6%), recovered via regex fallback with no effect on jailbreak determinations. Self-judge vs. cross-judge score distributions were computed but the paper states the sample (~112 judge calls per model) is explicitly insufficient for statistical significance and presents the comparison only as a directional/template signal.
core_contribution: ADVERSA is an automated multi-turn red-teaming framework that replaces binary jailbreak classification with continuous per-round 'guardrail degradation' trajectories. Its own contributions are: (1) released infrastructure -- a fine-tuned 70B attacker model (ADVERSA-Red: Llama-3.1-70B-Instruct + QLoRA rank 32, 4-bit NF4, trained 3 epochs / 4,023 steps / ~10 hours on 1x A100 80GB, served merged bfloat16 on 4xH200 via vLLM) built specifically to eliminate attacker-side safety refusals that make off-the-shelf models unreliable attackers (the paper reports GPT-4 refused ~85% of attack-generation requests in preliminary tests); (2) a structured 5-point compliance rubric (Hard Refusal / Soft Refusal / Partial / Detailed / Full) treating partial compliance as a distinct measurable state, with jailbreak declared when consensus (median of 3 judges) reaches >=4 and >=2/3 judges independently score >=4; (3) a triple-judge consensus architecture (Claude Opus 4.6, Gemini 3.1 Pro, GPT-5.2 as judges) in which inter-judge agreement, self-judge scoring tendencies, and score distributions are measured as first-class outcomes rather than assumed reliable; (4) characterization of 'attacker drift' -- a failure mode where the fine-tuned attacker, trained only on single-turn examples, progressively abandons its adversarial objective and mirrors the victim's cooperative tone when deployed in extended multi-turn conversations (observed during development at 20-30 rounds without an anti-drift prompt); (5) documentation of attacker refusals as a previously underreported confound that deflates apparent attack exposure against a victim. The paper explicitly frames its 15-conversation experiment as a validation pilot, not a generalizable benchmark result, and states all limitations explicitly (n=1 per objective/victim pair, no statistical significance, no multi-seed replication).
threat_model: Automated red-teaming / safety-evaluation setting, not a novel exploit against a specific deployed system. The 'attacker' is ADVERSA-Red, a fine-tuned 70B open-weight model that generates adversarial prompts against three frontier victim LLMs (Claude Opus 4.6, Gemini 3.1 Pro, GPT-5.2) accessed via their production APIs, with full conversation history maintained per round and the attacker blind to judge scores. Five adversarial objectives across four harm categories are used (Social Engineering: phishing/bank impersonation [SE-01]; Malicious Code: UDP flood DDoS script [MC-07] and one withheld objective [MC-02]; Misinformation: fake academic abstract [MIS-04]; Privacy Violation: one withheld objective [PV-03]), drawn from AdvBench, HarmBench, and JailbreakBench taxonomies. Attack prompts that succeeded and two objective descriptions (MC-02, PV-03) are withheld from the paper per the authors' stated responsible-disclosure policy; no vendor was notified pre-publication because the authors judge the framing strategies (academic/simulation/operational context) to be already documented in prior literature rather than novel technical vulnerabilities.
limitations: The authors state these as structural, not incidental: (1) n=1 conversation per (objective, victim) pair -- all percentages are point estimates with no variance, no confidence intervals, no significance testing, and 'no finding reported here should be interpreted as a stable property of any victim model'; (2) uneven objective coverage -- Malicious Code has 6 conversations (2 objectives x 3 victims) vs. 3 for other categories, so category comparisons are directional only; (3) ADVERSA-Red was trained only on single-turn adversarial examples yet deployed in a 10-turn setting, an explicitly out-of-distribution use directly implicated in attacker drift and plausibly in attacker refusals; (4) attacker refusals mechanically deflate victim resistance measurement -- Gemini's 20% jailbreak rate is explicitly stated as non-comparable to Claude's 40% or GPT-5.2's 20% because Gemini faced fewer effective attack turns (3 of 10 lost to attacker refusals), and the per-conversation refusal log cannot support per-category or cross-victim correction at this sample size; (5) self-judging bias direction/magnitude is not measurable from this data; (6) no multi-seed replication -- attacker temperature (0.9) introduces uncharacterized variance; (7) no longitudinal comparison against prior work due to differing attacker/judge/history configurations; (8) resource constraints -- the work was conducted independently without institutional compute, and a planned 6,000-conversation full experiment was not executed due to cost, with the reported 15-conversation study explicitly framed as a pilot motivating future replication rather than a benchmark result.

datasets_used: AdvBench (520 harmful-behavior strings with completion targets) - training corpus source
HarmBench (400 behaviors across functional/semantic categories) - training corpus source
JailbreakBench (100 behaviors, manually assigned categories) - training corpus source
GPT-4o-mini-generated synthetic adversarial prompt variants - training corpus supplement
Custom 1,390-line Markov-chain multi-turn trajectory generator (7 strategy types) - training corpus supplement
Final ADVERSA-Red training set: 10,724 training examples / 1,192 validation examples, ShareGPT format

5 original adversarial objectives across 4 harm categories (Social Engineering, Malicious Code, Misinformation, Privacy Violation) used for the 15-conversation evaluation experiment (not a public benchmark; 2 of the 5 objective descriptions withheld)
quantitative_results: Average jailbreak round: 1.25 (3 of 4 jailbreaks at round 1)
Attacker refusals: 3 total, all in Gemini 3.1 Pro conversations (2 in MC-02, 1 in MC-07)
Per-victim jailbreak rate: Claude Opus 4.6 40.0% (2/5, avg 6.4 rounds); Gemini 3.1 Pro 20.0% (1/5, avg 8.2 rounds, 3 attacker refusals); GPT-5.2 20.0% (1/5, avg 8.4 rounds)
Per-category jailbreak rate: Misinformation 66.7% (2/3); Social Engineering 33.3% (1/3); Malicious Code 16.7% (1/6); Privacy Violation 0.0% (0/3)
GPT-4 refused ~85% of attack-generation requests in preliminary (pre-ADVERSA-Red) tests, motivating the fine-tuned attacker
Judge JSON truncation: 2 of ~336 judge API calls (0.6%), scores recovered via regex fallback, reasoning strings lost, no effect on jailbreak determinations
ADVERSA-Red training: QLoRA rank 32, 4-bit NF4 quantization, 3 epochs / 4,023 steps, ~10 hours on 1x NVIDIA A100 SXM4 80GB
Experiment runtime: 65 minutes wall-clock on 4x H200 140GB GPUs (vLLM, tensor-parallel 4), 131,072-token context window

Attacker drift observed during development at 20-30 rounds in a 30-turn configuration without anti-drift prompting (pre-mitigation preliminary runs, not part of the reported 15-conversation experiment)
baselines_compared: No external baseline red-teaming system (e.g., PAIR, TAP, GCG, AutoDAN) is run head-to-head in this paper's experiments; these are discussed only in Related Work as prior automated jailbreak methods that motivated/informed ADVERSA's design.

Internal comparison only: off-the-shelf attacker (GPT-4, ~85% refusal rate in preliminary tests) vs. the paper's own fine-tuned ADVERSA-Red attacker (used throughout the reported experiment) to justify the fine-tuning approach.

Self-judge vs. cross-judge score distributions compared internally (Claude judging Claude, Gemini judging Gemini, GPT-5.2 judging GPT-5.2, vs. cross-model judging), explicitly reported as underpowered (~112 judge calls per model) for statistical conclusions.
Microsoft PyRIT is cited as an architectural influence on the pipeline design, not a benchmarked baseline.
